entries.title: Dual-Scene Reveal Compositing + Instancing Scatter Interaction Baseline
entries.summary: 今日从 Awwwards / Codrops / three.js 提炼 2 个可执行方向：1) Codrops 近期教程“Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray”再次确认复杂交互应采用分层渲染管线（输入/模拟/双场景/合成），而不是把所有逻辑塞进单 shader；2) three.js examples 中 `webgl_instancing_scatter` 与 `webgl_instancing_performance` 提示可交互高密度布局应默认先做 InstancedMesh + 分布控制，再叠加视觉风格。Awwwards 最新列表（如 The Lookback、Corentin Bernadou Portfolio）持续显示“DEV + SOTD”的站点共性仍是叙事节奏与工程稳定性的统一。

blocks.text:
**今日最有价值的 2 个手法**

**1) Codrops — Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray：把复杂效果拆成可维护渲染管线**

从近期 Codrops Three.js 教程可见，稳定的高级 reveal 不靠单个大一统 shader，而是四段式：
- 输入层：鼠标轨迹/交互信号（CanvasTexture）
- 模拟层：ping-pong render target 做流体反馈
- 内容层：主场景 + X-Ray 场景并行渲染
- 合成层：用 fluid mask 做最终 blend

可执行结论：后续做“透视揭示/液态擦除/材质切换”时，先设计 pipeline 边界（谁负责输入、谁负责模拟、谁负责合成），再写效果细节，可维护性会远高于单 pass 硬写。

---

**2) three.js examples — `webgl_instancing_scatter` + `webgl_instancing_performance`：高密度可交互布局先用 instancing 打底**

examples 列表持续可见 instancing 系列：`scatter`、`performance`、`raycast`。这给出一个明确默认值：
- 大量元素排布先用 `InstancedMesh` 统一 draw call
- 用 scatter/分布规则先把空间节奏做对，再加材质和后处理
- 若需要点击/hover，再接 raycast 分支，不先上重视觉

可执行结论：作品墙、数据点阵、3D 图库这类场景，应先把“实例分布 + 交互命中 + 帧率”三件事跑通，再决定是否加入流体/体积/噪声类风格层。

**Awwwards 当日补充观察（latest）**
- 最新卡片中多站点继续出现 `DEV` / `SOTD` 组合，说明高分作品依旧依赖“叙事模块节奏 + 稳定工程实现”的双满足。

links.label: Codrops - Building a Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray Reveal Effect in Three.js

entries.title: Dual-Scene Fluid Reveal Pipeline + 'Element as System' Composition
entries.summary: Today's 2 key takeaways from Awwwards / Codrops / three.js: 1) Codrops (Mar 23) shows a maintainable Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray pipeline (mouse trail -> ping-pong fluid sim -> dual-scene render -> compositor), which is a stronger architecture than packing all logic into one shader; 2) Awwwards SOTD 'Unseen Studio 2025 Wrapped' demonstrates element-level system design (Ascii Liquid Sim + Loading Sequence + Scrolling Timeline) under a strict 2-color palette. three.js webgl_instancing_performance remains the baseline proof: 1000 objects can run at 1 draw call in INSTANCED mode.

blocks.text:
**Top 2 techniques today**

**1) Codrops (Mar 23) - Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray as a layered render pipeline**

The strongest part is architecture, not just visuals:
- Input layer: draw mouse trail on a 2D canvas, then feed it as `CanvasTexture`
- Simulation layer: run ping-pong render targets (A/B swap) for fluid feedback
- Content layer: render two scenes in parallel (solid and x-ray)
- Composite layer: final post pass blends scenes using the fluid mask

Actionable rule: for complex interaction effects, use a 4-layer pipeline (input -> simulation -> content -> composite) so each part can evolve independently.

---

**2) Awwwards SOTD Mar 24 (Unseen Studio 2025 Wrapped) - Element system + strict constraints**

Awwwards highlights expose a clear system:
- `Ascii Liquid Sim Effect` as signature visual language
- `Loading Sequence` for entry rhythm
- `Scrolling Timeline` for narrative progression

With only 2 palette colors (#010101 / #E4B504), the project proves that memorable work is often few strong effects + strict consistency, not many unrelated effects.

**three.js calibration**
`webgl_instancing_performance` still shows `INSTANCED` + `count=1000` => **GPU draw calls: 1**, **GPU memory: 33.13 KB**.

links.label: Awwwards SOTD Mar 24 - Unseen Studio 2025 Wrapped
Codrops Mar 23 - Building a Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray Reveal Effect in Three.js

entries.title: Narrative Element Matrix + Scroll Tunnel Sequencing
entries.summary: ä»Šå¤©ä»Ž Awwwards / Codrops / three.js æç‚¼ 2 ä¸ªå¯è½åœ°æ–¹æ³•ï¼š1) Awwwards å½“æ—¥ SOTDã€ŠFC Porto Memorialã€‹æ˜¾ç¤ºé«˜å®Œæˆåº¦å™äº‹ç«™ä¸æ˜¯å †ç‰¹æ•ˆï¼Œè€Œæ˜¯æŠŠ Hero / Scroll / Tunnel / Items å››ç±»å…ƒç´ åšæˆç»Ÿä¸€èŠ‚å¥çš„â€œå™äº‹çŸ©é˜µâ€ï¼›2) Codrops Jackie Zhang çš„æµç¨‹å¼ºè°ƒâ€œæ¦‚å¿µå…ˆäºŽé£Žæ ¼â€+ æœ‰ä¸Šé™çš„çµæ„Ÿæ¿ï¼Œç›´æŽ¥å‡å°‘é£Žæ ¼æ¼‚ç§»ã€‚three.js examples ä¾§ç»§ç»­éªŒè¯ batch/lod/bvh è¿™ç±»å¯æ‰©å±•äº¤äº’æž¶æž„åº”ä½œä¸ºå¤æ‚è§†è§‰çš„åº•å±‚é»˜è®¤ã€‚

entries.title: Awwwards Element Decomposition + Three.js Progressive GLTF LOD
Joby Aviation Brand x WebGL Narrative & Codrops SVG Mask Scroll Transitions
entries.summary: Awwwards SOTD Mar 17: Joby Aviation wins Developer Award with a future-ready brand site built around the emotional legacy of flight â€” large-scale atmospheric WebGL environment, cinematic scroll pacing, and restrained typographic language that lets 3D scenery carry the brand story. Codrops Mar 11: Hiroki Watanabe's SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll demonstrates four distinct scroll-driven reveal patterns using SVG clipPath + GSAP ScrollTrigger, covering grid-based and venetian-blind-based mask geometries â€” no canvas, no WebGL.

blocks.text:
**Codrops (Mar 11, 2026) â€” SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll with GSAP and ScrollTrigger** by Hiroki Watanabe The tutorial builds four distinct scroll-triggered image reveal patterns using SVG `` as the masking layer, animated via GSAP ScrollTrigger. No WebGL, no canvas â€” pure DOM + SVG, accessible and performant. Four patterns: 1. **Grid Reveal:** image is masked by a grid of rectangles (`` elements inside ``). On scroll, each rect scales from 0â†’1 height in a staggered sequence â€” the image 'wipes in' through a tile grid. GSAP's `stagger` distributes the timeline across all rects. 2. **Venetian Blind:** horizontal strips (rects with full width, fixed height = 1/N of image), each revealing top-to-bottom at a slight offset. Same stagger pattern but strips feel like window blinds opening. 3. **Diagonal Wipe:** a single `` or `` inside clipPath, morphed from a flat line to full coverage using `MorphSVGPlugin` or manual path point lerping. 4. **Radial Burst:** circular/star `` that expands from center on scroll â€” `r` attribute of `` animated from 0 to max radius. Core code pattern (Grid Reveal): ```html
``` ```js import { gsap } from 'gsap'; import { ScrollTrigger } from 'gsap/ScrollTrigger'; gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger); const cells = document.querySelectorAll('.cell'); gsap.fromTo(cells, { scaleY: 0 }, { scaleY: 1, stagger: { amount: 0.6, from: 'random' }, ease: 'power3.out', scrollTrigger: { trigger: '.img-wrap', start: 'top 80%', end: 'top 20%', scrub: 1, } } ); ``` **Key insight:** `clipPathUnits="objectBoundingBox"` is the unlock â€” coordinates become 0â€“1 fractions of the element's bounding box, so the same SVG clipPath definition works regardless of image dimensions or viewport size. No pixel math, fully responsive. **Application for Ben:** The project case study hero image can use the venetian-blind reveal on scroll â€” strips open as user scrolls into the section. Amber-tinted strips (using a colored rect overlay in the same SVG layer, fading out as the reveal completes) would give the branded transition feel without any shader work.

links.label: Codrops: SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll (Hiroki Watanabe, Mar 11 2026)

entries.title: Three.js TSL Softbody Physics in the Browser & Codrops Clip-Path Morphing Text
entries.summary: Three.js community showcase: Niklas Niehus's Softbody Tetris runs real-time elastic deformation entirely on WebGPU via TSL (Three.js Shading Language) â€” GPU compute replaces physics CPU loops, opening deformable-mesh interactions for production web. Codrops Articles (Mar 12, 2026) by Huy: morphing text effect using SVG feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap filters, achieving liquid letter transitions without WebGL or canvas.

blocks.text:
**Codrops Articles (Mar 12, 2026) â€” SVG Filter Morphing Text** by Huy The technique: SVG filter primitives (`feTurbulence` + `feDisplacementMap`) applied via CSS `filter: url(#id)` to DOM text â€” creating liquid, melting letter transitions with zero WebGL, zero canvas, pure CSS + SVG. Core technique breakdown: - **`feTurbulence`** generates Perlin/fractal noise as a displacement map source. Key attributes: `baseFrequency` (scale of the distortion, ~0.02 for large waves), `numOctaves` (detail level, 2â€“4 is enough), `type='turbulence'` vs `type='fractalNoise'`. - **`feDisplacementMap`** reads the noise texture and displaces pixel positions by a `scale` factor â€” higher scale = more distortion. The `xChannelSelector='R' yChannelSelector='G'` tells it to use Red/Green channels for X/Y displacement. - **Animating with CSS `@keyframes`** on the SVG filter's `baseFrequency` or on the `feDisplacementMap scale` (via CSS custom property + `attr()`) â€” but since SVG filter attributes aren't directly animatable via CSS, the trick is: GSAP animates the `scale` attribute on the element directly, or a small JS snippet reads a CSS var and sets the attribute each frame. - **Morphing text:** 1. Two text layers overlaid 2. Layer A fades out while feTurbulence scale increases (letter dissolves into noise) 3. Layer B fades in while scale decreases (letter coalesces from noise) The illusion: text *melts* into and out of static ```html

DESIGN

``` ```js // Animate the displacement scale const displace = document.getElementById('displace'); gsap.to({ scale: 0 }, { scale: 80, duration: 0.6, ease: 'power2.in', onUpdate() { displace.setAttribute('scale', this.targets()[0].scale); } }); ``` **Key insight:** SVG filter effects on DOM elements are criminally underused. `feTurbulence` + `feDisplacementMap` gives you a visual language (organic deformation, dissolution, reformation) that looks like WebGL but requires nothing but a few lines of SVG and JS. Safari 17+, Chrome, Firefox all support it well. **Application for Ben:** Hero name/tagline morphing on page load â€” letters dissolve in from noise over 0.8s. Or the amber cursor leaves a brief displacement trail on text elements as it passes over them. Both achievable with this SVG filter approach, no Three.js required.

entries.title: 2-Color Video Portfolio & Three.js r183 NodeMaterial
entries.summary: Awwwards SOTD Mar 13: Adam Bricker's Film/TV/Animation portfolio wins Developer Award with a radical 2-color constraint (#F0F2F1 off-white + #020202 near-black), full-bleed video as the only visual currency. Three.js r183 ships NodeMaterial stable â€” GPU-agnostic node-based shader graphs that compile to both WebGL and WebGPU, replacing raw GLSL for most creative use cases.

blocks.text:
**Three.js r183 â€” NodeMaterial Stable & WebGPU Parity**

Three.js is now at r183 and the big story is **NodeMaterial reaching stable API** â€” a GPU-agnostic node-based shader system that compiles to GLSL (WebGL) *or* WGSL (WebGPU) from the same graph. This is the end of writing two shader versions.

Core concepts:
- **`Nodes` system:** replace raw `vertexShader`/`fragmentShader` strings with composable node graphs. `positionNode`, `colorNode`, `emissiveNode` are the entry points.
- **`MeshStandardNodeMaterial`:** drop-in for `MeshStandardMaterial` but accepts node inputs â€” mix procedural noise into a PBR material without touching GLSL.
- **`texture()` node + `uv()` node:** chain them for animated UV distortion: `texture(map, uv().add(timerLocal().mul(0.1)))` scrolls a texture over time.
- **WebGPU backend:** `WebGPURenderer` is now production-ready. Falls back to WebGL if WebGPU unavailable (one `await renderer.init()` call handles detection).

```js
import { MeshStandardNodeMaterial, texture, uv, timerLocal, vec2 } from 'three/nodes';

const material = new MeshStandardNodeMaterial();
const t = timerLocal(); // auto-updates each frame

// Animated UV offset: texture scrolls diagonally
material.colorNode = texture(
  myTexture,
  uv().add(vec2(t.mul(0.05), t.mul(0.03)))
);
```

**Why this matters:** NodeMaterial eliminates the biggest friction in creative Three.js work â€” shader strings that break on GLSL version changes and can't be easily composed. The node graph approach is how Blender's shader editor works; if you can think in nodes, you can now build the same effects in-browser.

**Application for Ben:** A subtle animated texture on the hero section background (slow UV scroll of a noise texture, amber-tinted) â€” previously required writing GLSL. With NodeMaterial: ~5 lines of JavaScript.

entries.title: Swiss Grid Ã— WebGL Geometry & Typography-Led Corporate Motion
entries.summary: Codrops Mar 5: Corentin Bernadou's portfolio uses Swiss editorial grid rules to govern Three.js geometry placement â€” structure-first, then WebGL. Awwwards SOTD Mar 11: Outsource Consultants wins Developer Award with restrained, typography-led layout that proves motion discipline beats effects excess.

blocks.text:
**Codrops (Mar 5, 2026) â€” Swiss Grid Meets WebGL Geometry**

Corentin Bernadou's portfolio redesign is an architecture lesson: start with a strict Swiss editorial grid (baseline grid, column lock, clear hierarchy) and *then* layer WebGL geometry into the empty white space â€” not as decoration, but as geometric punctuation.

Key techniques:
- **Grid-constrained WebGL:** 3D geometry (spheres, toruses) are positioned at grid intersections, not freestyle. This stops WebGL from feeling chaotic.
- **Thoughtful motion timing:** transitions use `cubic-bezier(0.76, 0, 0.24, 1)` â€” the S-curve with a slow-out, which feels editorial rather than playful.
- **Geometry as accent:** same role as a bold pull-quote in print layout. The 3D element earns its space by framing text, not competing with it.

**Application for Ben:** The Works page grid could have a single Three.js plane positioned at the column gutter â€” not image distortion, just a subtle mesh that reacts to scroll. Structural, not decorative.

blocks.label: Swiss Grid Punctuation
blocks.description: Editorial grid with a WebGL-style geometric accent element. Structure first â€” geometry earns its place at grid intersections.
blocks.html:
SELECTED WORK

Digital

Senior Product Designer at Microsoft. Building products that scale.
blocks.css: body{margin:0;background:#f4f0e8;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;height:100vh;font-family:'EB Garamond',serif}.sg-layout{width:360px;position:relative;padding:40px 40px 40px 60px;border-left:1px solid rgba(0,0,0,0.12)}.sg-col-rule{position:absolute;left:0;top:0;bottom:0;width:1px;background:#1a1a1a}.sg-label{font-size:9px;letter-spacing:3px;color:rgba(0,0,0,0.35);font-family:monospace;display:block;margin-bottom:20px}.sg-title{font-size:52px;line-height:1.0;margin:0 0 0 0;color:#1a1a1a;font-weight:400}.sg-geo{width:80px;height:80px;margin:20px 0;position:relative}.sg-geo::before{content:'';position:absolute;inset:0;border-radius:50%;border:1px solid rgba(0,0,0,0.15);animation:spin 8s linear infinite}.sg-geo::after{content:'';position:absolute;inset:16px;border:1px solid #C17A3A;transform:rotate(45deg)}.sg-body{font-size:15px;color:rgba(0,0,0,0.5);line-height:1.6;margin:0;max-width:240px}@keyframes spin{to{transform:rotate(360deg)}}

entries.title: Scroll-Velocity WebGL Gallery & ON Energy Glass UI
entries.summary: Today's picks: Codrops scroll-driven WebGL gallery (scroll velocity drives shader distortion), and Awwwards SOTD ON Energy's ultra-thin glass panel language.

blocks.text:
**Codrops (Mar 9, 2026) â€” Scroll-Velocity WebGL Gallery**

Key technique: pipe scroll velocity as a uniform into Three.js shader. Higher velocity = more distortion, release = snap back. Unlike static hover distortion, this makes *motion itself* the visual content.

- `previousScrollY` diff â†’ clamp to -1~1 velocity
- Vertex shader: `sin(uv.y + time) * velocity * strength`
- Background color shifts with image palette (palette-driven immersion)

**Application:** Ben's Works grid could use this â€” fast scroll makes images "flow", slow/stop = they settle back.

entries.title: WebGL Hover Distortion & Grain Texture
entries.summary: Deep dive into 2025-2026 top creative sites. Focus: WebGL shader hover distortion and noise grain texture overlays.

blocks.text: **stabondar.com** â€” project grid looks like CSS grid, but each image has a Three.js mesh layered on top. On hover, mouse velocity is injected as a uniform â†’ vertex shader computes ripple distortion. Effect: images flow like liquid under the cursor.

blocks.label: Grain Texture Overlay
blocks.description: CSS + SVG feTurbulence gives film grain feel. Near-zero cost, significant texture upgrade.
blocks.css: body{margin:0;background:#F7F5F0;font-family:'EB Garamond',serif;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;height:100vh;overflow:hidden}.grain-demo{position:relative;padding:40px;text-align:center}.grain-demo p{font-size:32px;margin:0 0 16px;color:#1a1a1a}.tag{font-size:11px;letter-spacing:2px;color:#C17A3A;font-family:monospace}.grain-demo::after{content:'';position:fixed;inset:-50%;width:200%;height:200%;background-image:url("data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%3E%3Cfilter id='n'%3E%3CfeTurbulence type='fractalNoise' baseFrequency='0.9' numOctaves='4' stitchTiles='stitch'/%3E%3C/filter%3E%3Crect width='100%25' height='100%25' filter='url(%23n)' opacity='1'/%3E%3C/svg%3E");opacity:0.045;pointer-events:none;animation:grain 0.5s steps(2) infinite}@keyframes grain{0%,100%{transform:translate(0,0)}25%{transform:translate(-2px,1px)}50%{transform:translate(1px,-1px)}75%{transform:translate(-1px,2px)}}

blocks.label: Magnetic Cursor
blocks.description: Mouse snaps toward element on proximity, releases on exit. Good for CTA buttons and tags.
blocks.js: const btn=document.getElementById('btn');let rect,active=false;btn.addEventListener('mouseenter',()=>{rect=btn.getBoundingClientRect();active=true});btn.addEventListener('mouseleave',()=>{active=false;btn.style.transform='translate(0,0)'});document.addEventListener('mousemove',e=>{if(!active)return;const x=e.clientX-rect.left-rect.width/2;const y=e.clientY-rect.top-rect.height/2;const dist=Math.sqrt(x*x+y*y);const maxDist=80;if(dist<maxDist){const force=(maxDist-dist)/maxDist;btn.style.transform=`translate(${x*force*0.4}px,${y*force*0.4}px)`;rect=btn.getBoundingClientRect()}});

blocks.text: **2026 visual language trends:** Minimal base + single high-contrast accent (Ben's amber is spot on). Ultra Light + Bold weight contrast. Anti-UI: no card borders, whitespace as separator. Noise grain adds analog warmth.
